title: Citizen Knope

jokes.text: It's an experimental fabric called bumble-flex. It's made out of synthetic bees' wings.
jokes.setup: Leslie asks about Chris's jogging shirt, expecting normal athletic wear details
jokes.punchline: Chris reveals it's made from synthetic bees' wings, a completely absurd fabric concept
jokes.explanation: Perfect character comedy - Chris's obsession with cutting-edge fitness tech taken to absurd extreme. 'Synthetic bees' wings' is wonderfully specific and impossible.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Chris, that's an interesting jogging shirt. What's it made of?

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Synthetic bees' wings.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, what?

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Synthetic bees' wings. It's moisture-wicking, hypoallergenic, and it makes a subtle humming sound when you run, which I find very motivating.

jokes.text: This is a nasal spray. Give me the flash drive. Give me the nasal spray.
jokes.setup: Chris demands Leslie's flash drive, suggesting a tense standoff
jokes.punchline: Leslie offers to trade a nasal spray instead, deflating the drama with mundane object
jokes.explanation: Simple misdirection gag that works through anticlimax. Leslie's attempt to negotiate with random office supplies is in character.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Give me that flash drive, Leslie.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I can't do that, Chris.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Hand it over. Now.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What if I offered you this nasal spray instead?

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: What? No. I want the flash drive.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It's a really good nasal spray. Clears you right up.

jokes.text: Leslie Knope! I am much faster than you! I have bumble-flex!
jokes.setup: Chase scene where Chris catches Leslie
jokes.punchline: Chris credits his speed to the ridiculous bumble-flex fabric from earlier
jokes.explanation: Good callback that builds on the absurdist setup. Chris's sincere belief in his ridiculous fabric enhances the comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I caught you! You know what? I have to credit my speed to that ridiculous bumble-flex fabric from earlier.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, really?

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: No, I'm just in really good shape.

jokes.text: I'm inventing a new spice called 'sal-gar.' It's part salt, part sugar.
jokes.setup: Leslie needs to keep busy during suspension
jokes.punchline: Her solution is inventing a pointless spice that's just salt and sugar mixed
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie character moment - her need to be productive leads to completely unnecessary innovation. The portmanteau 'sal-gar' is nicely crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I need to keep busy during this suspension. I've decided to invent a new spice.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It's salt and sugar mixed together. I'm calling it... Sweetalt.

jokes.text: What could that possibly be good on? Butterscotch pudding.
jokes.setup: Ben asks what sal-gar could possibly be used for
jokes.punchline: Leslie immediately has a specific, weird answer ready
jokes.explanation: Clean setup-punchline. The specificity of butterscotch pudding sells Leslie's weird confidence in her invention.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: What could sal-gar possibly be used for?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, it's a salt-vinegar hybrid that I developed for preserving pickled eggs in a brine solution, but I also use it as a natural de-icer for the front steps, and I've found it works great as a mild exfoliant when mixed with pumice stone.

jokes.text: Why didn't I resign in disgrace 12 years ago?
jokes.setup: Ben reflecting on exploring private sector opportunities
jokes.punchline: He wishes he'd been disgraced sooner to discover these opportunities
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Ben's tendency to overthink and find silver linings in bad situations.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking. If I'd been publicly humiliated and forced out of government earlier, I could've discovered the private sector sooner. I could've had years of corporate success instead of wasting my time actually serving the public.

jokes.text: You might want to stop saying 'resigned in disgrace.' Especially during job interviews.
jokes.setup: Ben keeps saying he 'resigned in disgrace'
jokes.punchline: Leslie points out this is terrible interview strategy
jokes.explanation: Straightforward observational humor. The obviousness is the joke - Ben needs this pointed out.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ben, you can't keep saying you 'resigned in disgrace.' That's a terrible interview strategy.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Well, that's what happened.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I know, but you have to spin it. Say you 'left to pursue other opportunities' or 'decided to explore new challenges.'

jokes.text: Mouse Rat, certified gold! Over 100 copies sold in Pawnee!
jokes.setup: Andy receives what appears to be a gold record
jokes.punchline: It's 'certified gold' for selling only 100 copies in a small town
jokes.explanation: Excellent subversion of music industry standards. The specific number '100 copies' in small-town context makes Andy's excitement both sweet and ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, this is a gold record? I'm certified gold?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: It's 'certified gold' for selling 100 copies in Eagleton.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That's... that's not how gold records work.

jokes.text: I know you're sad you couldn't get tickets to the 'Watch the Throne' tour, so I got you a watch and a tiny throne instead.
jokes.setup: Tom wanted concert tickets but couldn't get them
jokes.punchline: Leslie literally interprets the album title as separate objects
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that perfectly captures Leslie's literal-minded thoughtfulness. The visual of a tiny throne is inherently funny.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I can't believe I missed out on those tickets. I've been trying to get them for weeks.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, I got you something even better. I got you the album 'Physical' by Dua Lipa.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Leslie, that's not... that's not a concert ticket.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: No, but it's physical. You can touch it. And I also got you this lump of clay I sculpted myself, because the album is called 'Physical' and this is a physical object.

jokes.text: She even took out the dumb clock part and put this in... 'Baller Time.'
jokes.setup: Tom shows off his customized watch
jokes.punchline: Leslie removed the functional clock and replaced it with just the words 'Baller Time'
jokes.explanation: Great character beat - Leslie's over-the-top personalization removes the watch's basic function. Tom's appreciation makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Check out my customized watch.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I removed the functional clock and replaced it with just the words 'Baller Time'.

jokes.text: These are the Black-eyed Peas. And I finally killed them.
jokes.setup: April shows her gift from Leslie
jokes.punchline: It's literally black-eyed peas (the food) that she's 'killed'
jokes.explanation: Perfect April moment combining her deadpan delivery with dark humor. The pun works on multiple levels and the delivery sells it completely.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: It's literally black-eyed peas. You killed them.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I thought you said you wanted to kill Black Eyed Peas!

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: The band, Leslie. The band.

jokes.text: Every year, I give Leslie the same present I give everyone: A crisp $20 bill. And every year, she gets me something thoughtful and personal. It makes me furious.
jokes.setup: Ron describes his gift-giving philosophy
jokes.punchline: He's angry at Leslie for being more thoughtful than his lazy approach
jokes.explanation: Excellent character work. Ron's irritation at being out-gifted despite doing the minimum is perfectly in character and relatable.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I don't believe in gifts. A man should provide for himself. If you can't buy it yourself, you don't need it.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, I got you something.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I don't want it.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You don't even know what it is.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Doesn't matter. I didn't get you anything, and I'm not going to start now just because you're making me look bad.

jokes.text: She had it installed over the weekend. [Ron looking at elaborate bathroom setup]
jokes.setup: Ron reveals Leslie's gift for him
jokes.punchline: It's an elaborate bathroom installation, overwhelming Ron with its thoughtfulness
jokes.explanation: Visual payoff to Ron's gift anxiety. The scale of Leslie's gesture (bathroom installation) justifies his overwhelmed reaction.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Leslie, this is... this is extraordinary. A full bathroom renovation?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I know how much you value your privacy and personal space. I wanted you to have the most beautiful bathroom in Pawnee.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I... I don't know what to say. This is the most thoughtful thing anyone has ever done for me.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You deserve it, Ron. You're one of my best friends.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: You've made me very emotional. I'm going to go sit in my new bathroom and think about my feelings in silence.

jokes.text: Aw, jeez. Socks. She gets me.
jokes.setup: After elaborate gifts for everyone else
jokes.punchline: Jerry gets basic socks but is genuinely touched
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry moment - he's happy with the least elaborate gift. The contrast with others' gifts and his sincere gratitude works well.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: And Jerry, we got you... socks.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh... well, these are... these are really nice socks. Thank you. This is very thoughtful.

jokes.text: And Elizabeth, I got you a Knope 2012... Menorah...Maybe? No. No, okay. Then I got you a tree ornament.
jokes.setup: Leslie tries to be culturally sensitive with gifts
jokes.punchline: Her uncertainty about the menorah and quick pivot to ornament is awkward
jokes.explanation: Mild cringe humor from Leslie's well-intentioned but clumsy cultural awareness. The hesitation and backtrack feel authentic.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I got you this beautiful menorah for Hanukkah. Actually, wait—is that offensive? I don't want to assume your religion. Here, take this ornament instead.

jokes.text: In fact, while I was in that meeting, I was also planning my mother's birthday party in my head. Strawberry margaritas! Another use for sal-gar!
jokes.setup: Leslie can't relax or take a beat as advised
jokes.punchline: She was multitasking during the meeting and found another use for her invented spice
jokes.explanation: Good callback that reinforces Leslie's inability to single-task. The sal-gar callback feels natural and builds the running gag.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, I'm relaxing. I'm taking a beat. See? Beat. I'm on it.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, I just realized I can alphabetize my spice rack while I'm taking this beat. Oh! And I invented a new spice. It's called Leslie's Essence. It's just paprika mixed with my DNA.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Turns out it's also great for removing stubborn stains!

jokes.text: Well, they call me 'the Swiss Army Accountant.' They don't call me that.
jokes.setup: Ben boasts about his versatile accounting skills
jokes.punchline: Immediately admits nobody actually calls him that nickname
jokes.explanation: Classic self-deflating humor. Ben's immediate honesty after trying to sound impressive is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I'm like a Swiss Army knife of accounting. I can do taxes, payroll, budgeting, forensic accounting...

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: People call me the 'Accounting Swiss Army Knife.'

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Nobody actually calls me that.

jokes.text: Calc-you-later.
jokes.setup: Ben ending a professional interview
jokes.punchline: He uses a terrible calculator pun as a farewell
jokes.explanation: Intentionally bad pun that works because it's so perfectly awkward for Ben's character. The cringe is the comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Well, I think that covers everything. Thanks for your time.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I guess you could say this interview really... added up.

jokes.text: We do not get a lot of humor here, and when we do, it's wonderful. Ted! Come in here! Say it to Ted when he comes in.
jokes.setup: Ben makes his calc-you-later pun
jokes.punchline: The interviewer is so starved for humor he calls in another person to hear the pun
jokes.explanation: Great escalation that makes Ben's bad pun funnier by showing how desperately the accounting firm needs humor.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Calc-you-later.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. Did you just make a pun? Hold on.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: Hey! Hey, come here! You gotta hear this!

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: This guy just said 'calc-you-later.' Calc-you-later!

jokes.text: Did you just scoop it out of the jar with your hand like a bear? Yes.
jokes.setup: Donna has marshmallow fluff on her hand
jokes.punchline: Tom's specific bear comparison and Donna's unapologetic confirmation
jokes.explanation: Good visual comedy with Tom's specific animal comparison. Donna's matter-of-fact response enhances the absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Donna, you have marshmallow fluff on your hand. You look like a bear that just raided a campsite.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: I know.

jokes.text: My latest attempt to drag this department into the 20th century. Tom, it's the 21st century. I know. I'd settle for getting you into the 20th.
jokes.setup: Tom says he's bringing them into the 20th century
jokes.punchline: When corrected it's the 21st, he says he'd settle for the 20th
jokes.explanation: Clean setup-punchline that works as both self-deprecating humor and commentary on the department's resistance to change.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm bringing you guys into the 20th century.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: It's the 21st century.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'd settle for the 20th.

jokes.text: 'Ben'... And much larger, 'Ann.' She definitely loves Ann.
jokes.setup: Tom shows a word cloud of Leslie's most-used words
jokes.punchline: Ann's name appears much larger than Ben's, indicating Leslie talks about Ann more
jokes.explanation: Nice visual gag that reinforces Leslie's friendship priorities. The awkward revelation adds character depth.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Look at this word cloud of Leslie's most-used words.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Oh my god, my name is huge! It's bigger than Ben's!

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yeah, Leslie talks about you way more than she talks about Ben.

jokes.text: We make a gingerbread version of the office. That's so good! I think the wood model is-- and we can decorate our offices with candy canes and gumdrops! That would be so adorable! Thanks, Ron! That's such a cute idea!
jokes.setup: Ron suggests a practical wood model
jokes.punchline: April suggests gingerbread instead and everyone loves it, crediting Ron with the 'cute' idea
jokes.explanation: Great character moment where Ron gets credit for April's whimsical idea. The irony of calling Ron's contribution 'cute' works perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I suggest we build a practical scale model out of wood.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Or we could make it out of gingerbread.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: That's a great idea!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Ron, that's so cute!

jokes.text: You're putting an awful lot of sal-gar on your pasta. This is just sugar.
jokes.setup: Ben notices Leslie adding sal-gar to her pasta
jokes.punchline: Leslie reveals she's just using straight sugar instead
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the sal-gar gag. Leslie's desperation leading to using pure sugar on pasta is absurd but in character.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, what are you putting on your pasta?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh, it's sal-gar. You know, salt and vinegar mixed together for that perfect savory-sour combo.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That's... that's not a real thing, is it?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, okay, fine. It's just sugar. Straight sugar.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, why would you put sugar on pasta?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Because I love it.

jokes.text: I can hear you breathing, and, yes, you can finish her pasta. Thank God. I'm so hungry. Mmm, it tastes like froot loops.
jokes.setup: Leslie leaves her sugar-covered pasta
jokes.punchline: Andy immediately eats it and notes it tastes like cereal due to the sugar
jokes.explanation: Nice payoff to the sugar pasta setup. Andy's specific taste comparison (Froot Loops) and Leslie knowing he'd want it works well.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: This tastes like cereal.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's my pasta.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Not anymore.

jokes.text: Like the drug PCP, we are so fast-acting and powerful, we should be illegal.
jokes.setup: Someone questions the PCP acronym for their committee
jokes.punchline: Leslie doubles down with an elaborate drug comparison
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie moment - taking a terrible acronym and defending it with ridiculous enthusiasm. The drug comparison is surprisingly elaborate.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Okay, so yes, the acronym is PCP. Parks and Creation Program. I know what you're thinking, but I've already looked into this. We're not changing it. Do you know how many letterheads we'd have to reprint?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: And frankly, I think the drug comparison is unfair. PCP is destructive and dangerous, whereas our Parks and Creation Program is constructive and... well, also dangerous, but in a good way. We're dangerously committed to this city's parks.

jokes.text: How amazing can he be if he got suspended? Pretty damn amazing, Diane. Now shush.
jokes.setup: Diane questions Leslie's competence due to suspension
jokes.punchline: Leslie defends herself in third person then tells Diane to shush
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Leslie's inability to maintain her cover. The third-person defense and abrupt 'shush' works.

dialogue.speaker: Diane
dialogue.line: You were suspended. How can I trust you're competent?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Leslie Knope is very competent. Leslie Knope has won numerous awards and commendations.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Shush.

jokes.text: See? His arms are crossed because he's mad at all the other marshmallow workers for annoying him. You like it? It's fine.
jokes.setup: Andy shows off his marshmallow Ron creation
jokes.punchline: Ron's characteristically understated response to Andy's enthusiastic explanation
jokes.explanation: Perfect character interaction. Andy's detailed explanation of marshmallow Ron's psychology contrasted with real Ron's minimal response is spot-on.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Look at this! I made you out of marshmallows! See, here's your hair, here's your face, and here's your little Ron body!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I don't like this.

jokes.text: When I was trying to decide how to decorate my office, I had to ask myself, 'what kind of candy should I choose?' Obviously chocolate, 'cause I'm sweet, smooth, and the ladies love me.
jokes.setup: Tom explaining his candy office decoration choice
jokes.punchline: His self-aggrandizing reasoning for choosing chocolate
jokes.explanation: Standard Tom vanity moment. The elaborate self-comparison to chocolate is in character but not particularly original.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I chose chocolate because it's the most valuable commodity in the world. It's more sought after than gold, more addictive than heroin, and it makes you feel like you're in a European commercial where people are really into it.

jokes.text: I didn't have time to make it into anything, and please don't eat it 'cause it cost $55 an ounce.
jokes.setup: Tom chose expensive gourmet fudge for his decoration
jokes.punchline: Reveals the absurd price and warns against eating his decoration
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Tom's priorities - he'd rather spend $55 on office candy than make effort. The specific price point sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: This is a twelve-hundred dollar box of artisanal fudge from a chocolatier in Belgium. It's not for eating. It's for decoration only.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: If you eat it, you will ruin the entire aesthetic of my office, and also you will owe me twelve hundred dollars.

jokes.text: But they do not make silver m&ms, so I spray-painted them. Okay, those are poisonous, so no one eat them.
jokes.setup: April explains her silver M&M desk decoration
jokes.punchline: She spray-painted regular M&Ms, making them toxic
jokes.explanation: Perfect April moment - her casual creation of poisonous candy delivered with complete deadpan. The matter-of-fact warning is brilliantly dark.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I spray-painted regular M&Ms to make them silver. They're toxic now.

jokes.text: Go throw up. I didn't eat any. Go throw up. Throw up.
jokes.setup: Concern about the poisonous M&Ms
jokes.punchline: Everyone telling someone to throw up despite them not eating any
jokes.explanation: Simple escalation gag. The repeated 'throw up' commands despite no actual consumption works as absurdist panic.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: You need to throw up right now!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: But I didn't eat any of them.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Throw up anyway, just to be safe.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: We're not taking any chances here. Throw up.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: The sooner you vomit, the better.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I really don't think I need to—

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Jerry, please, for all of us, just throw up!

jokes.text: I like you-- I've always liked you-- but I also like Eddie. Ed--who--who's Eddie? Eddie's my current CFO, but I might fire him and hire you.
jokes.setup: Dennis seems to be offering Ben a job
jokes.punchline: Reveals Eddie is his current CFO who he might fire, creating awkward situation
jokes.explanation: Good cringe setup establishing Dennis as inappropriately casual about firing people. Ben's confusion sells the awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis Feinstein
dialogue.line: Ben, I'd like to offer you a position as my CFO.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That's... that's a great opportunity, Dennis. Thank you.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis Feinstein
dialogue.line: Well, I just need to fire my current CFO first.

dialogue.speaker: Eddie
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Dennis Feinstein
dialogue.line: Sorry Eddie, nothing personal. Ben's just better at... everything.

jokes.text: You got kids? No. That's good. Dum-dum Eddie has two kids.
jokes.setup: Dennis asks if Ben has kids
jokes.punchline: He's relieved Ben doesn't, calling current employee 'dum-dum Eddie' who has kids to support
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about firing someone with kids. Dennis's casual cruelty and the 'dum-dum' nickname create effective cringe.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis Feinstein
dialogue.line: You got kids?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: No, I don't.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis Feinstein
dialogue.line: Oh thank God. I was worried I'd have to pay you more. Unlike that dum-dum Eddie, he's got three kids to support.

jokes.text: Don't talk to Eddie. Treat him like you would treat a person in another country that you paid $25,000 to hunt.
jokes.setup: Dennis tells Ben not to acknowledge Eddie
jokes.punchline: Compares it to treating someone you paid to hunt in another country
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark and specific analogy. The casual reference to human hunting is so absurd it becomes comedy gold. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis Feinstein
dialogue.line: Don't acknowledge him. Just... pretend he doesn't exist. It's like when you pay someone to hunt in another country and they come back and try to talk to you about it.

jokes.text: There is no strategy to chutes and ladders. It's just luck. My daughter's five. Well, your daughter is an idiot.
jokes.setup: Parent suggests chutes and ladders for the rec center
jokes.punchline: Leslie calls a five-year-old an idiot for playing a luck-based game
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie moment - her competitive nature leads to insulting a child. The escalation from game critique to calling a 5-year-old an idiot is absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Parent
dialogue.line: Why don't we just set up Chutes and Ladders in the rec center?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Chutes and Ladders? That's a game of pure luck. You know what? You're an idiot.

dialogue.speaker: Parent
dialogue.line: I'm sorry?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, how old are you?

dialogue.speaker: Parent
dialogue.line: I'm thirty-seven.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Then you're definitely an idiot.

jokes.text: Speaking of which, the fact that yahtzee is not in the rec center is a tragedy on par with human trafficking. Slight exaggeration...
jokes.setup: Leslie discussing rec center improvements
jokes.punchline: Compares lack of Yahtzee to human trafficking, then mildly acknowledges the exaggeration
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation showing Leslie's tendency to catastrophize minor issues. The mild self-correction makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We need to get a Yahtzee game for the rec center. The lack of Yahtzee is basically human trafficking.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, that's not human trafficking. But it's bad.

jokes.text: God, Diane, will you stop it with the letter-writing campaign? No.
jokes.setup: Diane suggests letter-writing again
jokes.punchline: Leslie's exasperated rejection of Diane's repeated suggestion
jokes.explanation: Simple running gag with Diane's one-note character. Leslie's frustration sells the repetitive nature.

dialogue.speaker: Diane
dialogue.line: You know, Leslie, letter-writing is really making a comeback. We should start a letter-writing campaign.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Diane, no. We are not doing a letter-writing campaign. We tried that last time, and it didn't work. We're not doing it again.

jokes.text: But there are only 12 of us. 13. Look! Our numbers are growing!
jokes.setup: Citizen points out they only have 12 people for ambitious plans
jokes.punchline: Leslie celebrates one additional person as significant growth
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Leslie's relentless optimism. Celebrating 8% growth as 'numbers growing' is perfectly delusional.

dialogue.speaker: Citizen
dialogue.line: We only have 12 people for all of this?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, we had 11 yesterday, so that's growth! That's a 9% increase!

jokes.text: I can handcraft 17-foot canoes out of western red cedar, but I can't glue two damn Graham crackers together!
jokes.setup: Ron struggling with gingerbread house construction
jokes.punchline: Contrasts his advanced woodworking skills with inability to handle simple candy construction
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy. The specific detail (17-foot canoes, western red cedar) contrasted with graham crackers is perfectly Ron. Great quotable line.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I have built entire cabinets from reclaimed wood. I have constructed a canoe from scratch. But this... this gingerbread house is destroying me.

jokes.text: My pleasure. See you in hell!
jokes.setup: Leslie gives Chris a thoughtful gift
jokes.punchline: Immediately switches to battle mode with 'See you in hell'
jokes.explanation: Perfect juxtaposition of Leslie's kindness and militancy. The abrupt tonal shift from gift-giving to declaring war is perfectly executed.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: See you in hell.

jokes.text: You're polling at 1%. What? No. That can't be right.
jokes.setup: Leslie expecting to hear her polling results
jokes.punchline: She's at an impossibly low 1% and can't believe it
jokes.explanation: Solid setup-punchline with the absurdly low number. Leslie's denial reaction is predictable but works.

dialogue.speaker: Advisor
dialogue.line: Your polling numbers came back.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Great! How am I doing?

dialogue.speaker: Advisor
dialogue.line: You're at 1%.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: 1%? That's impossible! I've shaken hands with at least dozens of people!

jokes.text: You know, the last delicious sip of a milkshake at the bottom of the metal milkshakey thing?
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to put positive spin on 1% polling
jokes.punchline: Elaborate metaphor about the last sip of a milkshake being the best part
jokes.explanation: Great Leslie moment showing her desperate optimism. The specific milkshake metaphor and 'milkshakey thing' phrasing is endearing.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You know what? One percent is actually great. It's like when you're drinking a milkshake and you get to that last sip at the bottom? That's the best part. That's the thickest, most concentrated milkshake experience. So one percent of voters? Those are my best sips.

jokes.text: I'm gay. Well, never mind then.
jokes.setup: Leslie accuses advisor of having affair with Ben
jokes.punchline: Advisor reveals he's gay, deflating Leslie's accusation
jokes.explanation: Clean misdirection gag. Leslie's matter-of-fact 'never mind' response to being corrected works well.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You're having an affair with Ben!

dialogue.speaker: Advisor
dialogue.line: What? No, I'm gay.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: B-b-b-b-Ben-jammin'!
jokes.setup: Jean-Ralphio greeting Ben
jokes.punchline: Turns Ben's name into a DJ Fresh Prince reference
jokes.explanation: Standard Jean-Ralphio wordplay. The DJ Fresh Prince reference fits his character but isn't particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Yo, it's DJ Ben Fresh, Prince of Bel-Air!

jokes.text: When she rips it off, she smiles, and it makes you feel things. Throw my name, I get a referral discount.
jokes.setup: Jean-Ralphio talking about his Brazilian wax
jokes.punchline: Oddly romantic description of painful procedure, then asks Ben for referrals
jokes.explanation: Good Jean-Ralphio moment combining TMI with his hustler mentality. The romantic description of pain is appropriately bizarre.

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Dude, I got a Brazilian wax last week. It was like a thousand tiny angels ripping the hair out of my body one by one, whispering sweet nothings in my ear.

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Hey Ben, you know anyone who does that? I need a referral.

jokes.text: Are you nuts? This is probably the first time you've had off since you've been kid president. True or false? True.
jokes.setup: Jean-Ralphio commenting on Ben's career choices
jokes.punchline: References Ben's past as teen mayor and Ben confirms he hasn't had time off since
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Ben's backstory. Jean-Ralphio calling it 'kid president' and Ben's honest confirmation works well.

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Dude, you were a teen mayor? That's the coolest thing I've ever heard.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Yeah, it was pretty intense.

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: So like, have you had any time off since then?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Not really, no.

jokes.text: If you don't love what you do... Then why do it? Then she ripped the hair from my b-hole.
jokes.setup: Jean-Ralphio sharing philosophical wisdom from his waxer
jokes.punchline: Immediately follows profound advice with graphic description of the waxing
jokes.explanation: Excellent juxtaposition of profound life advice with crude reality. The tonal whiplash is perfectly Jean-Ralphio.

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: My waxer told me something really profound. She said, 'The only way to grow is to shed what's holding you back.' Pretty deep, right?

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Anyway, then she ripped all the hair off my butt. I mean ALL of it. I could see things I didn't know existed down there.

jokes.text: Your suspension's been lifted. Please, come back to work. Thank you.
jokes.setup: Chris gives Leslie a Christmas present
jokes.punchline: The gift is just ending her suspension early
jokes.explanation: Nice anticlimax after elaborate gift-giving. Chris's practical approach to gift-giving contrasts with Leslie's thoughtfulness.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Merry Christmas, Leslie. I got you something special.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my god, Chris, thank you! What is it?

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I'm ending your suspension early. You can come back to work tomorrow.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's... that's the best Christmas present ever.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, I'm pregnant. Wait, what? No, that's not it.
jokes.setup: Ben says he has something to tell Leslie in person
jokes.punchline: Blurts out fake pregnancy announcement then immediately corrects himself
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection gag. Ben's awkward blurting and immediate correction feels authentic to his nervous character.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I'm pregnant!

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: No, no, no—Leslie is pregnant. I'm not pregnant. I can't get pregnant.

jokes.text: Turns out, I cannot make a gingerbread house, which would bother me if I were an 8-year-old girl.
jokes.setup: Ron admitting he can't make gingerbread houses
jokes.punchline: Says it would only bother him if he were an 8-year-old girl
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron line dismissing his failure at a craft project. The specific demographic comparison is both funny and in character.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: It would only bother me if I were an 8-year-old girl.

jokes.text: This year, though? My friends won. In fact, I got my ass handed to me.
jokes.setup: Leslie talking about gift-giving as competition
jokes.punchline: Admits her friends beat her at gift-giving for once
jokes.explanation: Nice character moment showing Leslie's competitive nature and genuine appreciation. The 'ass handed to me' phrasing fits.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I have to admit, my friends beat me at gift-giving for once. Ann got me this beautiful scarf, Tom got me a vintage record player, and Ben got me this incredible watch. I was devastated. I couldn't compete. So I went to my office, I cried for twenty minutes, and then I got back to work.

jokes.text: So I have no idea what you're talking about. Don't know what Quickbooks are.
jokes.setup: Jean-Ralphio's resume claims Quickbooks experience
jokes.punchline: Immediately admits he has no idea what they are
jokes.explanation: Good Jean-Ralphio moment showing his complete lack of qualifications despite confidence. The honesty after lying is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm really good with Quickbooks.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: What experience do you have with Quickbooks?

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: None. I have no idea what that is.

jokes.text: You want to talk about spreading and sheets, we can go back to my place and I will rock your-- You're fired!
jokes.setup: Boss mentions spreadsheets
jokes.punchline: Jean-Ralphio turns it into sexual innuendo and gets immediately fired
jokes.explanation: Predictable Jean-Ralphio sexual wordplay leading to immediate consequences. In character but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Boss
dialogue.line: I need everyone to review these spreadsheets carefully.

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Spreadsheets? More like spread... sheets, if you know what I'm saying. Those are gonna be all over me tonight.

dialogue.speaker: Boss
dialogue.line: Jean-Ralphio, you're fired. Get out of my office.

tmdb_overview: Unable to get work done from inside the Parks Department, Leslie forms a citizen action group and fights from the outside. Meanwhile, everyone in the office brainstorms ideas for what to get Leslie for Christmas.
insight: Citizen Knope leans hard on character comedy and absurdist escalation despite middling impact scores.
